business_info: Urea nitrogen (UN) just within the borderline of passing refers to UN = 29

In the thrombosis prediction pipeline, patient IDs are not re-used across merges, but a patient can have more than one external identifier in upstream feeds; only the internal ID is stable for reporting and audits.
Sex is occasionally backfilled from admission paperwork; if a conflict is found between visits, the most recently captured value is displayed in dashboards even though the raw history is retained.

Birthdays in the patient registry may be rounded to the first of the month for privacy in certain exports; the operational dataset keeps the exact date but downstream extracts may show the rounded value without a flag.
The lab subsystem ingests multiple panels per blood draw; when that happens, several lab rows share the same collection timestamp but differ in analyte coding.

Urea nitrogen is sometimes labeled as BUN or UUN depending on the instrument interface; internally these get mapped during ETL, but ad-hoc extracts may show the instrument label rather than the mapped label.

“Borderline of passing” is a QA phrase used by nursing staff during protocol training; it generally refers to values near a cut score, but the exact cut score can vary by analyte and by the version of the training slide deck.

Some lab results are stored with an “<” or “>” qualifier from the analyzer (e.g., “<0.2”); for cohorting work, teams typically strip the qualifier into a separate field during analysis rather than attempting to cast the original string.
A “clean lab row” is one that has a populated collection date, a non-null result, and a verified flag; unverified rows are kept for traceability but are often excluded from model feature generation.
Clinical teams sometimes refer to “onboarding date” as the day the first sample was collected, even if the patient record existed earlier due to pre-registration.

The model training extracts apply a “first-year rule” to reduce instrument drift, meaning they prefer early-year measurements when duplicates exist; this is a convention rather than an enforced constraint.

For thrombosis surveillance, lab timestamps are treated as local hospital time; daylight-saving changes were not normalized in the older interface feeds, so hour-level ordering can look odd around clock changes.
A small subset of patients are marked as “registry-only” because they were entered for administrative tracking; they may have demographics but no completed lab panels.

When cohorts mention “standard evaluation,” some analysts interpret it as the same window used for quarterly validation runs; the quarter boundaries have changed historically when reporting cadence changed.
Core program participants often receive a second lab draw after an initial abnormality; operationally, coordinators track participation in a spreadsheet rather than in the database.
Some sites use “CMP” to mean “core monitoring protocol,” which is not the same as the Comprehensive Metabolic Panel; email threads often confuse the two.
For patient age calculations in quality reports, the organization uses “age at index test” rather than age today; the index test is whichever lab the analyst has chosen as the anchor for the report.
Some dashboards bucket ages into “<30”, “30–50”, “50–70”, “70+”, but the bucket definition is not consistently documented as inclusive/exclusive at the boundaries.
The thrombosis model’s “patient summary” view prefers a single sex value per patient even if historical visits differ; a tie-breaker is applied based on the most recent update timestamp when present.
A “monitoring cycle” is informally counted from the first lab in a sequence until 30 days after the last lab; sequences that overlap are sometimes merged by coordinators during follow-up calls.
An “evaluation period” in slide decks usually refers to the time window used to compute baseline rates; some teams exclude the first 14 days after enrollment to avoid capturing acute-care labs.

Ages in narrative notes are occasionally entered as ranges (e.g., “late 20s”) and then later corrected; the correction can lag behind lab ingestion, so demographics and labs can temporarily appear inconsistent.
The lab QA team uses a “pass/fail” label for certain education materials, but it is not the same as a clinical reference range; it’s meant for data completeness checks.
Some urea nitrogen results are captured in mg/dL and others in mmol/L depending on the site; the integration guide recommends normalizing, but not all downstream tables are guaranteed normalized.
Duplicate patients can occur when name/DOB matching fails at intake; de-duplication is a manual process and may not be reflected immediately in analytics extracts.

For ad-hoc cohort requests, analysts often filter to “reportable labs” which are those released to the patient portal; internal-only labs may exist for calibration and do not reflect patient physiology.

An “index lab” is the lab record selected for per-patient summarization; different teams pick different index labs (first ever, first in year, most recent, or highest-risk panel) depending on the report.
The patient “birthday” field should represent date of birth, but in some legacy loads it was temporarily populated with an estimated birth year (e.g., 1970-01-01) when the day/month were unknown.
Some sites treat “enrolled” as having signed consent, while others treat it as having a first completed lab; the program office has not standardized the wording across all communications.
A “core” label is sometimes used to describe the set of vitals and labs required for the baseline risk score; missing any one of the core elements can mark the visit as incomplete.

The thrombosis dataset has occasional “batch dates” that reflect when a lab was loaded rather than when it was collected; unless explicitly stated, analysts should prefer collection/observation dates for temporal logic.

Occasionally, a patient’s demographic record is created after the first lab is received (e.g., emergency intake); in these cases, lab records may precede the first visible demographic timestamp by hours or days.

For sensitivity analyses, some teams exclude patients with only point-of-care labs because those devices round results differently; the rounding is considered acceptable clinically but complicates threshold-based filtering.
